title: Overtime Pay Calculator
shortDescription: Calculate your overtime pay from hours, hourly rate and multiplier.
description: Quickly work out how much extra you earn for overtime. Enter your overtime hours, your hourly rate and the overtime multiplier (such as 1.5 for time-and-a-half or 2 for double time) to see your total overtime pay instantly.
metaDescription: Free overtime pay calculator. Enter overtime hours, hourly rate and multiplier (1.5x, 2x) to instantly see how much extra you earn on your paycheck.

result.label: Overtime pay
result.empty: Enter your overtime hours, hourly rate and multiplier to see your overtime pay.

sections.heading: How overtime pay is calculated
paragraphs: Overtime pay is the extra money you earn for hours worked beyond your normal schedule. The formula is simple: overtime hours multiplied by your hourly rate, multiplied by the overtime multiplier.
Common multipliers are 1.5 (time-and-a-half) and 2 (double time). For example, 5 overtime hours at $20/hour with a 1.5 multiplier equals $150 in overtime pay.

sections.heading: When overtime rates apply
paragraphs: Many regions require employers to pay a higher rate once an employee exceeds a set number of hours per week or per day. Holidays and night shifts may carry an even higher multiplier.
Always check your contract or local labor law for the exact threshold and rate that apply to your situation.

howTo.name: Enter overtime hours
howTo.text: Type the number of overtime hours you worked.

howTo.name: Enter your hourly rate
howTo.text: Add your normal pay rate for one hour of work.

howTo.name: Set the multiplier
howTo.text: Choose the overtime multiplier, such as 1.5 or 2, to see your total overtime pay.

faq.q: What is the overtime pay formula?
faq.a: Overtime pay = overtime hours × hourly rate × multiplier. For example, 4 hours × $25 × 1.5 = $150.
Time-and-a-half means you are paid 1.5 times your normal hourly rate for each overtime hour.
Double time means a multiplier of 2, so each overtime hour pays twice your normal hourly rate.
No. It shows gross overtime pay before any taxes or deductions are taken out.
Yes. Enter any multiplier required by your contract or local law, such as 1.25, 1.5 or 2.
